Roger Brent Chosen to Lead Scientific Research at the Molecular Sciences Institute.

[BERKELEY, CALIFORNIA ­ January 1, 2001] The Board of Trustees of the Molecular Sciences Institute of Berkeley, California, announced that Dr. Roger Brent has been appointed as the Scientific Director and a member of the Board of Trustees of the MSI, effective November 15, 2000. Dr. Sydney Brenner, founding Scientific Director of the MSI, will remain as its President and Chairman of the Board of Trustees.

The Molecular Sciences Institute is a nonprofit basic research institute. Its mission is to create a predictive biology, grounded in the knowledge of the inventory of biological parts and how those parts work together to create system outcomes. To this end the Institute emphasizes the creation of tools and techniques for generating biological information, the development of computational and analytical frameworks for integrating this data, and the design and engineering of biological systems. In accomplishing these goals, the Institute will significantly advance understanding of how living systems function and evolve, and will have positive effects on human health and agriculture.

As Scientific Director, Dr. Brent will lead the MSI's current research program and help to identify future avenues of research. In addition, he will work with and help develop the Institute's staff of independent scientists.

Dr. Brent stated, "The research program at the Molecular Sciences Institute is now impressive. We have made progress in learning how to weave together the work of biologists, mathematicians, computer scientists, and physicists to learn how genomic information is integrated to produce cells and organisms, and to apply this knowledge to the engineering of new biological systems. Future consequences of successful work will be quite important."

Dr. Brent, age 44, earned a bachelor's degree in mathematics and computer science from the University of Southern Mississippi and a Ph.D. in Biochemistry and Molecular Biology at Harvard University.

He joined the Harvard Medical School faculty in 1985 in the Department of Genetics and was also appointed to the scientific staff of the Department of Molecular Biology at Massachusetts General Hospital (MGH). He received the prestigious Pew Scholar Award in 1987 and a Senior Scholar Award from the Ellison Medical Foundation in 2000. He also advises numerous government, corporate, and philanthropic organizations. Dr. Brent left Harvard and MGH in January 1998 to help Dr. Brenner start the Molecular Sciences Institute in Berkeley.

After years of notable work on eukaryotic transcription factors and functional genomic methods, including developing research methods and tools used by thousands of labs throughout the world, Dr. Brent is now focusing on bringing about a more quantitative approach to biology. He is pushing for the development of new technology and tools to aid in faster and better biological data acquisition, for finding ways to convert this data into "computable" form, and for increasing collaborations between disciplines to help solve complex biological problems.
